data.title: Valmont Industries: Management's Plans Trump Short-Term Uncertainty
data.description:
One company that, quite honestly, is doing better than I expected it to right now is Valmont Industries, Inc. (VMI). For those not familiar with the business, it operates as a producer of certain products and a provider of certain services for the infrastructure and agricultural markets. On the infrastructure side, for instance, it offers up structures such as steel, prestressed concrete, and composites dedicated to the utility sector. They also offer up certain structures made of steel, aluminum, wood, and composites for the lighting and transportation markets. They offer up coatings and even provide certain telecommunications equipment. They even cater to the solar industry through their single-axis solar tracker. You also have, on the agricultural side, certain irrigation equipment, not to mention technology solutions. In fact, the company's network has more than 175,000 connected devices that help farmers detect field issues early on while also optimizing water and energy usage. There are other examples, but you get the idea.

In my last article about the company, published in May of 2025, I ended up calling it a ‘hold’ candidate. In retrospect, that was probably a mistake. The fact of the matter is that while the firm looks pretty much fairly valued as it stands right now, it is actually on a very positive track for the next several years. Management has a coherent growth strategy, and achieving that will result in material upside for shareholders. Relative to other similar firms, the stock is also attractively priced right now. In light of all of this, I believe that upgrading it to a soft ‘buy’ is the right move.

This is not to say that my opinion on the matter will not change as new data becomes available. It certainly could. It just so happens that, before the market opens on April 21st, management will be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. Leading up to that point, analysts believe that revenue and profits will improve. I still remain cautious when it comes to the end markets that the company caters to. But on the whole, I think that some optimism is appropriate.

Taking A Fresh Look At Valmont Industries

In the chart above, you can see financial performance for the company for the last three fiscal years. Revenue has, quite honestly, been rather lumpy. In fact, in 2025, sales increased only slightly from $4.08 billion to $4.10 billion. We did see a slight improvement in net profits from $348.3 million to $350.3 million. Cash flows, however, were mixed. Operating cash flow declined from $572.7 million to $456.5 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, on the other hand, we would get a rise from $444.4 million to $538.9 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA ended up pulling back slightly from $647.9 million to $638.8 million.

The best way to understand what is going on here is to look at both of its operating segments separately. The driver of growth from 2024 to 2025 was none other than its Infrastructure segment, which reported a 3% increase in revenue from $3.01 billion to $3.10 billion. Although the company saw weakness in certain categories such as lighting and transportation as well as solar, utility, and telecommunications, demand pushed sales up. Interestingly, North America was an area of strength for the business. International markets, on the other hand, worsened. The utility side of things was particularly robust, with sales jumping 10.4% year over year because of higher volumes and higher prices that the company was able to charge customers. Continued investments in things like data centers and grid capacity have been responsible for investments in electrical energy generation and distribution. This makes perfect sense if you ask me. Even more impressive ended up being the telecommunications side of the business, with revenue skyrocketing 25.2%, with the North American market benefiting from increased investments from carriers and the company snatching up market share through its quick-turn order strategy.

Even though revenue increased, profitability took a hit, with operating income falling from $497.1 million to $430.2 million. The good news is that this was primarily the result of management making the tough decision to incur an impairment charge of $89.4 million associated with its solar and access systems reporting units, as well as certain realignment charges amounting to $7.6 million. These are one-time events and non-cash in nature.

More difficult ended up being financial performance when it came to the Agriculture segment. Driven by an 11.3% plunge in North America, overall revenue for this unit dropped 5.8% from $1.08 billion to $1.02 billion. Management mentioned here that lower irrigation equipment sales reflected continued softness in the agricultural market. It didn't help that lower grain prices, trade policies, and the timing of government funding all negatively impacted its end customers. Some of this was also weather-induced. In 2024, there was an increase in the replacement of irrigation equipment because of severe weather events. That did not recur in 2025.

In the chart above, you can see historical data for the agricultural industry in the US. Total net farm income grew from $127.5 billion to $154.5 billion. This might look great at face value. But the big driver here was a surge in government funding from $10.1 billion to $30.5 billion. Actual crop cash receipts contracted from $244.9 billion to $238.1 billion. That marks the third consecutive year of weakness when it comes to crop receipts. And that is where the company is particularly impacted. Although for 2026, we are expected to see a slight improvement in cash receipts from $238.1 billion to $240.8 billion. Net farm income should fall from $154.5 billion to $153.4 billion. This is in spite of the fact that we should see a further rise of 45.2% when it comes to federal government direct farm program payments.

As revenue for the Agriculture segment dropped, operating profits plunged 33.4%, declining from $138.3 million to $92.1 million. Obviously, lower revenue negatively impacted the company. But in such an asset-intensive space with low margins, it's not unthinkable that the company would experience a contraction in its margins because it has a large amount of fixed assets that revenue must be spread between. Selling, general, and administrative costs also rose because a rise in expected credit losses in Brazil more than offset lower compensation and incentive costs.

When it comes to the 2026 fiscal year, management expects revenue to come in at between $4.2 billion and $4.4 billion. Unfortunately, the weakness in the agricultural market should worsen, leading revenue to fall to between $950 million and $1 billion for 2026. Infrastructure revenue, meanwhile, should grow to between $3.25 billion and $3.4 billion. On the whole, however, management expects earnings per share to jump from $20.50 to $23.50. At the midpoint, that would mean profits of $438.6 million. If we scale up other profitability metrics similarly, that would mean adjusted operating cash flow of $674.7 million and EBITDA of about $799.8 million.

Long term, management is optimistic about the company and its prospects. Even though there will be certain challenges in the near term, the firm is targeting continued expansion over the next few years. Between 2028 and 2029, for instance, the business expects revenue to grow by between $500 million and $700 million. In the chart above, you can see what net profits, adjusted operating cash flow, and EBITDA would be for the company at the low end, the midpoint, and the high end if management achieves these targets. This is based on management forecasting earnings per share of between $25 and $30 once these revenue targets are captured.

In the chart above, you can see what the annualized upside for the company would be if management can achieve that in the next three years. That would correspond with 2028. And in the chart below, you can see what the annualized upside would be if management takes four years to get to that goal. While there are scenarios where we would be looking at market returns or even below-market returns, most of these are scenarios where the company slightly exceeds or even comfortably beats the market, which can be expected to appreciate by between 10% and 11% per annum on average.

Relative to other similar companies, the stock is actually decently priced. In the chart below, you can see how the business compares to five other similar enterprises. Although on an absolute basis it is priced in the fair value category, it is cheaper than three of the five firms that I compared it to on a price-to-earnings basis. It's also cheaper than four of the five companies when it comes to both the price-to-operating cash flow approach and the EV-to-EBITDA approach.

Of course, my opinion could change as data justifies it. But the good news is that, at least in the near term, analysts expect further expansion from the company. They believe that the first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year will see revenue come in at $995.9 million. That would be up from the $969.3 million the business reported a year earlier. Earnings per share should also grow from $4.32 to $4.67. At the midpoint, this would mean net profits expanding from $87.3 million to $93.1 million. The table below shows certain cash flow metrics for the first quarter of 2025. In all likelihood, seeing revenue and profitability expand would mean experiencing a rise in most or all of these as well.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that's available right now, I would say that my previous stance on Valmont Industries was incorrect. The company is actually doing quite well considering how difficult the agricultural industry is. The stock is not exactly cheap right now. But if management can achieve the upside in terms of revenue and profitability it is forecasting, I would argue that shares justify enough upside to warrant a soft ‘buy’ rating at this time.

data.title: Sandisk: A Supercycle Is Still A Cycle
data.description:
From DRAM To NAND Flash Memory

After updating my outlook on Micron (MU) and the DRAM market in February 2026, I now turn my attention to Sandisk Corporation (SNDK), a pure-play developer and manufacturer of NAND flash memory. As indicated in the table below, DRAM represented 79% of Micron's total revenues in the second quarter of fiscal 2026, with NAND accounting for the remaining 21%. This is the key difference between Micron and Sandisk, as the latter is entirely focused on the NAND flash memory market.

The DRAM market is well-known for its deeply cyclical behavior and abrupt market turns. At the end of the day, DRAM is a commodity that goes from periods of oversupply to periods of shortages, as is the case today. There is no question that the DRAM market is in shortage today, but the real question is how long will it remain so?

So what is the difference between DRAM and NAND memory? To put it simply, NAND memory is used to store data, while DRAM becomes useful when retrieving that stored data. DRAM is a volatile form of memory, meaning that it loses its data when power is turned off, while it is the opposite for NAND. This is precisely why NAND memory is used in applications like solid-state drives, smartphones, USB storage devices, and SD cards, among many others.

In terms of market size, the NAND memory market is much smaller when compared to DRAM, even though they are both growing at a rapid pace. More precisely, the size of the global NAND memory market is expected to reach $58.7 billion in 2026, compared to an estimated $124.4 billion for the DRAM market. Given the significant surge in demand associated with the ongoing AI infrastructure boom, both markets are in a state of severe shortage, as reflected by surging spot prices across the board. Memory is now a critical bottleneck in the global AI data center supply chain, which allows both DRAM and NAND manufacturers to report all-time-high margins and profits.

In a sense, the global NAND and DRAM markets are highly similar in terms of global market share, with Samsung and SK Hynix dominating both industries. It is only when looking at the smaller players that the market structure starts to change. More specifically, the two largest manufacturers represented 49% and 68% of the global NAND and DRAM markets as of Q4-25, respectively. Despite its exclusive focus on the NAND market, Sandisk held a market share of only ~13% as of the fourth quarter of 2025, with its key joint-venture partner Kioxia slightly ahead at ~15%. Another point that I would like to highlight is the fact that Samsung, SK Hynix, and Micron all have vertically integrated supply chains with in-house fabs.

While Sandisk's partnership with Kioxia helps in lowering capital intensity, this strategy also carries drawbacks, especially in times of shortages like we are experiencing today. It is difficult to understate how critically important the partnership with Kioxia is for Sandisk. This collaboration takes place within an entity called Flash Ventures, owned at 49.9% by Sandisk. Key terms of the joint-venture agreement come to expiry as early as December 2029. As per Sandisk's 2025 10-K filing:

Kioxia owns the facilities and provides wafer manufacturing services to Flash Ventures at cost using manufacturing equipment owned or leased by Flash Ventures and process technologies co-owned or contributed by us and Kioxia. Flash Ventures accounts for approximately 80% of the total manufacturing capacity in the facilities owned by Kioxia.

In short, the NAND flash memory market is much smaller in size and also a lot more fragmented when compared to the DRAM market. Despite its pure-play exposure, Sandisk is the second smallest player in the industry, only ahead of Yangtze Memory Technologies, China's largest NAND manufacturer. Given its aggressive expansion plan, I would also expect YMTC to surpass Sandisk in terms of size over the next few quarters. As reported by Reuters on April 14th, the Chinese manufacturer is in the process of building three additional facilities, with each having a production capacity of 100,000 wafers per month. To put this number of 300,000 additional wafers per month into perspective, YMTC is currently operating two manufacturing facilities, which have a combined capacity of 200,000 wafers per month. With this 150% increase in production capacity, YMTC could leap ahead of Sandisk, Micron, and Kioxia once all the new facilities come online. The first factory is expected to start production later this year, reaching a capacity of 50,000 wafers per month in 2027. Although it is still a relatively small amount, it shows that Chinese manufacturers are serious about gaining market share, and that is true for both NAND and DRAM.

The S&P 500 Superstar

Over the last few weeks, I have spent quite a bit of time researching beaten-down names like Novo Nordisk (NVO), Copart (CPRT), ServiceNow(NOW), and Salesforce (CRM), among others. By looking into Sandisk today, I am doing the exact opposite, as the stock has been the S&P 500's best performer in both 2025 and 2026 year-to-date. In 2025 alone, Sandisk surged by an impressive 559%, on top of adding another 285% so far in 2026. When it started to trade as an independent entity back in February 2025, Sandisk was initially trading around $35.00 per share. Using today's share price of around $920.00, Sandisk has now delivered total returns in excess of 2,500% over a period of just 14 months or so. This is truly impressive, indeed.

As a reminder, Western Digital (WDC) announced its intention to separate into two publicly listed entities back in October 2023. This strategic decision came after the activist firm Elliott Management disclosed a stake in Western Digital in November 2022 and later put pressure on WDC's board of directors to explore such a separation. If we go further back in time, we also find that Western Digital completed the acquisition of Sandisk in May 2016, a deal worth ~$15.8 billion at closing. The timing of the transaction came just before the significant upswing in NAND and DRAM demand starting in 2017 and peaking in the following year, in 2018. To provide additional context, Sandisk is currently trading at a $135.6 billion market capitalization, which is ~8.6x higher than what Western Digital paid a decade ago.

Following the completion of Sandisk's spin-off in February 2025, Western Digital retained a 19.9% stake in the company. In early June 2025, WDC disposed of another large block of shares, representing 14.6% of SNDK's outstanding shares. As of early January 2026, Western Digital retained 7.5 million shares, or the equivalent of a 5.1% stake. More recently, in February 2026, Western Digital announced another disposition of 5.8 million shares at a public offering price of $545.00 per share. As of now, WDC still holds approximately 1.7 million Sandisk shares with the intention of completing the divestiture in subsequent transactions.

At the end of the day, how much value was truly created by Elliott's involvement for WDC's shareholders? Some could argue that no value was lost whatsoever if investors in Western Digital simply kept their shares of the spin-off, but how many really did so? Without questioning the initial rationale behind the separation, the 19.9% stake Western Digital retained following the spin-off would still be worth ~$27.0 billion as of now, or roughly 22% of WDC's current market capitalization of ~$122.6 billion. In terms of raw market capitalization, that is to say that the spin-off has now become larger than the parent.

I would like to conclude this section by saying that all the beaten-down stocks mentioned previously were all market darlings at some point. For example, Novo Nordisk (NVO) went from a top performer in 2023 and 2024 to being hated as of right now, with a drawdown from peak that has now reached ~75%. From market darling to the garbage bin in roughly two years. In previous articles, I have told a similar story about many other companies, with Trade Desk (TTD) and Brown-Forman (BF.B) being great examples. Yes indeed, they all operate in completely different industries with unique economics, but the core underlying pattern is the same, which is the cyclical nature of everything in financial markets. As Edward Dewey wrote in his book 'Cycles: The Science Of Prediction':

Anyone who starts out in business without knowing what time it is on the economic clock will obviously owe much to luck if he succeeds. And many men who have engaged successfully in business for a long time owe much of their sheer good fortune in having started out when the economic sun was rising.

Compared to all of those former darlings, Sandisk operates in an industry notorious for its deeply cyclical nature and abrupt market turns. There is no question that the NAND flash memory market is currently in a state of severe shortage, given the surging demand associated with the ongoing and historic AI infrastructure boom. How long is it going to last? And how long does it have to last to justify Sandisk's current market capitalization of $136.5 billion?

Using the Excel add-on Wisesheets, I have compiled Sandisk's historical financial statements going all the way back to fiscal 2022, when it was still a subsidiary of Western Digital. While the company is currently reaping the benefits associated with the AI supercycle, I think it is also important to keep in mind what the financial picture may look like in the midst of a downturn, like the one experienced over the last few years. For those wanting a more detailed look at Sandisk's historical financial statements, feel free to download the attached Excel file.

Valuation

Looking at a consensus of 18 analysts' estimates, Sandisk is expected to deliver earnings per share of $42.62 and $105.33 in fiscal 2026 and fiscal 2027, respectively. This shows that market participants are expecting a continued and significant acceleration in Sandisk's financial results over the next few quarters. In addition, management has guided for diluted earnings per share between $12.00 and $14.00 in the third quarter of 2026, which is equivalent to between $48.00 and $56.00 per share on an annualized basis. The street consensus for fiscal 2027 is approximately twice what Sandisk's management expects for Q3-26, meaning that earnings per share will have to double next year only to meet what is already priced in.

Using the street's EPS estimate of $105.33 per share in fiscal 2027, Sandisk is currently trading at a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 8.7x based on SNDK's closing price of $919.47 on April 16th. While this looks highly compelling at first sight, we have to remind ourselves that we are dealing with a highly cyclical industry in which significant capacity is expected to come online after fiscal 2027.

As mentioned previously, China's YMTC is expected to multiply its production capacity by 2.5x over the next few years, with the first facility expected to enter production later in 2026. Samsung is also expected to increase output at its Xi'an Plant 1 located in China, with plans to expand capacity at its Xi'an Plant 2 by about 40,000 wafers per month. SK Hynix also plans to boost production by up to 50,000 wafers per month at its Dalian Plant 2, also located in China. Last year, Kioxia also announced plans to double its NAND production capacity by fiscal 2029 with the expansion of its Yokkaichi and Kitakami facilities. In addition, Micron also announced the start of construction of its $100 billion mega-fab in the state of New York, one of the largest private investments in the history of the United States. To my knowledge, this will be one of the last projects to come online, as first production is not expected until 2030.

On the demand side, the historic AI infrastructure boom is still raging, but as discussed in my previous article about Blue Owl (OWL) and ARES Management (ARES), there is now trouble brewing in private credit, one of the key financing channels for the buildout of AI data centers. The initial public offerings of OpenAI and Anthropic are also coming up, signaling a key moment in this historic infrastructure boom. As I learned during my research on Microsoft (MSFT), Meta Platforms (META), and Oracle (ORCL), hyperscalers are already allocating a significant proportion of their operating cash flows toward the construction of AI data centers. How much more can they really invest, knowing that Oracle already put quite a bit of debt on its balance sheet?

With capital expenditures that are expected to peak only in fiscal 2028, ORCL's balance sheet is under increasing pressure and now carries net debt of ~$90 billion.

Over the years, I have been involved in a multitude of deeply cyclical industries, including mining, oil and gas, and maritime shipping, just to name a few. One thing I have learned is that you want to buy them when they are losing money every quarter, meaning when their earnings multiple is infinite, as there are no earnings. On the contrary, you want to sell them at peak earnings when their valuation multiples actually look cheap. In my view, this applies perfectly not only to Sandisk with NAND flash memory but also to Micron and the high-bandwidth memory market.

Sandisk is currently trading at approximately 8.7x the earnings expected in fiscal 2027. Considering all the production capacity expected to come online before the end of 2027, this could well be the year marking the top of this supercycle. If that is indeed the case, paying 8.7x peak earnings could end up being very expensive once the cycle turns.

Risk Factors

One of the biggest risks associated with my 'Strong Sell' rating relates to timing, as nobody truly knows when the shortage of NAND flash memory will peak. Over the next few years, Samsung, SK Hynix, Kioxia, and YMTC have all committed to significant ramp-up production to alleviate the current shortage. A good portion of this new production capacity is expected to come online in 2027 and 2028, with some projects not expected to enter production until 2030.

On the demand side, it is to wonder how much larger the current AI infrastructure boom can get. Private credit has been a key funding channel for the construction of AI data centers, and this space is now dealing with heavy redemptions across the entire ecosystem. On that note, Bloomberg recently reported that approximately half of all the data center projects in the United States have now been cancelled or delayed, in part due to shortages of key components.

Conclusion

In my view, the current shortages of NAND flash memory are likely to continue for the remainder of fiscal 2026 and to also persist well into fiscal 2027. However, beyond that point, I think we will start to see a commensurate response in supply and a demand picture that could also begin to cool off a little bit. Based on those supply and demand dynamics, fiscal 2027 could mark the peak of this NAND supercycle and, consequently, the peak of earnings for Sandisk.

My 'Strong Sell' rating reflects my view that Sandisk is currently offering an unattractive risk-reward proposition. With the share price up by 2,500% in just over a year and a street consensus expecting earnings per share to more than double between fiscal 2026 and 2027, how much upside remains? On the contrary, the downside potential is significant once the shortage of NAND memory alleviates and spot prices retreat from their high.

The higher Sandisk's share price goes, the more asymmetric the risk-reward proposition will become on the downside. However, with no significant new production capacity coming online until 2027, the shortage of NAND memory could still get worse in the near term. As usual, patience and prudence will be key here. And no, I don't think this time is different for memory stocks, as a supercycle is still a cycle. On the other side of this unprecedented uptrend, I expect the supply hangover to also be one for the ages, although this might only come as we move closer to 2030 and beyond.

data.title: CEF Insights: EMO - Opportunity In Structural Growth Of North American Energy
data.description:
CEFA:

Welcome to CEF Insights, your source for closed-end fund information and education, brought to you by the Closed-End Fund Association.

Today we are joined by Peter Vanderlee, Portfolio Manager with ClearBridge Investments. ClearBridge is an affiliate of Franklin Templeton and sub-advisor for ClearBridge Energy Midstream Opportunity Fund (EMO).

We are happy to have you with us today, Peter.

Peter Vanderlee:

Wonderful to be here. Thanks for having me.

CEFA:

Peter, I mentioned the ClearBridge Energy Midstream Opportunity Fund. Can you discuss the investment strategy of the fund?

Peter Vanderlee:

The ClearBridge Energy Midstream Opportunity Fund focuses on investing primarily in North American midstream energy companies, businesses that transport, store, and process oil, natural gas, and natural gas liquids. These are typically fee-based models, which means they generate stable cash flows with limited direct exposure to commodity price volatility. Our strategy emphasizes high-quality companies with strong balance sheets, durable asset footprints, and disciplined capital allocation. We're particularly focused on businesses that can generate sustainable free cash flow and return capital to shareholders through dividends and share buybacks. Importantly, the fund seeks to deliver a combination of attractive income and long-term capital appreciation, while also benefiting from structural growth trends in the U.S. energy production and export, especially in LNG.

CEFA:

How do you determine the specific security selections as you build your portfolio?

Peter Vanderlee:

Security selection is grounded in a bottom-up fundamental process. We focus on five key pillars that can be summarized as follows. Number one, balance sheet strength and leverage discipline. In practice, this means low to moderate leverage ratios for the companies we invest in. Our companies also typically have a rating of investment grade. Number two, quality and strategic value of assets, such as pipelines, processing infrastructure assets, storage facilities, and export capabilities. We look for assets that are strategically located, servicing attractive basins, and that are difficult to replace. Number three, cash flow stability and growth potential. We favor companies with strong, stable, and recurring free cash flow, which also have the ability to exhibit growth. The cash flow stability stems from contractual terms that favor take-or-pay provisions, minimum volume commitments, and long-term duration of contracts with financially sound counterparties. The growth potential stems from built-in contractual inflation escalators, as well as the ability to process more hydrocarbons, translating into higher fees collected. We tend to shy away from companies that exhibit a large dependence on commodity prices, as those prices tend to be volatile, compromising cash flow stability and complicating the stable dividend policy. Number four, strong and attractive dividend profiles. In terms of the dividend, we look for a company's ability to sustain the dividend at a minimum, which typically translates into a conservative payout ratio or generous coverage ratios. We also look for a company's ability to grow the dividend over time, providing a measure of inflation protection. We abhor distribution or dividend cuts and carefully scrutinize the ability of the company to, at a minimum, sustain the dividend and better yet, the ability of that dividend to grow over time. Number five, management quality and capital allocation track record. We prefer seasoned management teams that have a proven track record of creating shareholder value over time. We assess management's capital allocation policy and scrutinize its track record in terms of its ability to navigate through different economic cycles and market environments. The goal is to own businesses that can navigate short-term volatility while compounding value over the long term. Outside of these fundamental factors, we look for opportunities where the stocks are trading at attractive prices, on an absolute basis and relative to their growth and income potential.

CEFA:

What factors or events would lead you to make significant changes to your portfolio?

Peter Vanderlee:

Portfolio changes are typically driven by a few factors. One is fundamental shifts in a company's outlook, such as a deteriorating balance sheet or weaker than expected cash flows. Another one is valuation dislocations, where stock either reaches or surpasses our estimate of fair value, or where more compelling opportunities exist elsewhere. A third one is changes in industry dynamics, including regulatory developments, M&A activity, or mutations in supply-demand, where the competitive dynamics will be shifting. And a fourth one is macro developments, such as changes in energy policy, interest rates, or global demand trends, especially as they relate to long-term competitive dynamics. We also continuously reassess relative opportunities within the midstream sector, reallocating capital towards companies that we believe, and in our view and analysis, have and exhibit the best risk-reward profiles.

CEFA:

Peter, the Federal Reserve has held rates steady. Markets have seen some volatility with shifts in U.S. trade policy, as well as geopolitical tensions. Where do you see the investment markets currently, and what is your outlook for the rest of 2026?

Peter Vanderlee:

Markets have been navigating a mix of crosscurrents, such as moderating inflation, stable interest rates, and ongoing geopolitical uncertainty. Within that environment, energy, and particularly midstream, has remained relatively resilient. Looking ahead, we're constructive, U.S. energy production continues to grow, and demand, especially for natural gas, is supported by increases in LNG exports and structural drivers, such as AI-driven power consumption. Midstream companies are well-positioned to benefit from this backdrop, with strong free cash flow generation, improving balance sheets, and growing shareholder returns.

CEFA:

What are the most significant risks you consider in the current environment?

Peter Vanderlee:

Key risks include commodity price volatility, which can impact sentiment even if midstream cash flows are more insulated; regulatory and political uncertainty, particularly around pipeline permitting and energy exports, geopolitical risks, which can influence global supply-demand dynamics, and microeconomic slowdowns, which could dampen energy demand. Now, that said, the sector today is much more resilient than in prior cycles due to the improved capital discipline and stronger financial positioning. More broadly, however, it's important to consider market risks. Midstream energy companies fit squarely within the “Halo”, hard asset, low obsolescence framework. And in the current environment, represents a particularly attractive place to be if the AI-driven tech trade continues to unwind. At the core of the Halo thesis is asset durability. Midstream businesses own physical infrastructure assets such as pipelines, storage facilities, and processing plants, which have extremely long, useful lives and have limited technological obsolescence risk. According to our analysis, one major point of differentiation of Halo stocks from technology stocks is the long, useful lives of the products they provide in contrast to assets like PCs, servers, or GPUs, that face rapid replacement cycles and uncertain competitive positioning over a five to six-year time horizon. This is in stark contrast to energy pipelines, for example, which have useful lives over 35 years. Now, this distinction is critical. Long-lived infrastructure assets are supportive of long-term contracts. Which in turn underpins highly predictable cash flows. That predictability is exactly what becomes scarce and valuable during a tech unwind. If the AI party fades under the weight of stretched valuations, heavy CapEx, and uncertain returns, investors tend to rotate towards businesses with stable earnings, tangible assets, and more reasonable valuations. We are witnessing that this shift is now on the way, with leadership broadening out and away from mega-cap AI-dominated tech companies towards sectors like energy and utilities that exhibit predictable earnings, strong free cash flow underpinned by tangible assets. Midstream stands out even within the Halo framework because it combines this defensiveness with structural growth. Natural gas demand, driven in part by AI-related power needs and LNG exports, provides a multi-year volume tailwind. These dynamics support sustained volume growth for high-quality midstream operators tied to both domestic demand and global energy security. In other words, midstream is not just hiding from tech risk. It is indirectly benefiting from the physical build-out required by AI. Finally, from a portfolio construction standpoint, midstream stocks offer diversification precisely when it's most needed. Halo-heavy groups like midstream exhibit low correlations to high-growth AI equities and provide free cash flow cushions and income stability in an AI-driven correction scenario. That combination of low correlation plus durable cash generation makes midstream a hedge against the derating of long-duration tech assets. In short, midstream companies embody the Halo playbook, long-lived hard-to-replace assets, contract-backed cash flows, and exposure to real physical assets with corresponding demand that exhibits low cyclicality. In a market increasingly questioning the level of AI-driven returns, that's not just the defensive positioning. It's also strategically advantaged and well-positioned to play offense.

CEFA:

How is the EMO portfolio currently positioned?

Peter Vanderlee:

The portfolio is positioned towards high-quality, large-cap, midstream operators, with strong balance sheets, attractive assets such as pipelines, storage facilities, processing plants, and export capabilities in the energy midstream space. Towards stable, recurring, and growing cash flows, with attractive dividend profiles, meaning an attractive upfront yield and the ability to demonstrate income growth over time. We emphasize companies that can generate copious amounts of free cash flow and return those to shareholders in the form of an attractive dividend, while also benefiting from long-term volume growth tied to U.S. production and exports.

CEFA:

How did the portfolio perform with the volatility we have experienced over the past several months?

Peter Vanderlee:

Despite broader market volatility, in part due to the ongoing Iranian conflict, our midstream portfolio has held up well. The midstream energy sector is well insulated from geopolitical uncertainty and actually, is a beneficiary. First, the conflict underscores the importance of U.S. energy independence. Second, as energy infrastructure is attacked or damaged in the Middle East, it will induce higher production volumes in the U.S. which the midstream sector benefits from. Third, as we're seeing from the Ukrainian conflict, international natural gas flows are being rerouted, especially around Europe, which is looking to reduce its gas dependence on Russia. This benefits the U.S. natural gas value chain, including many midstream companies, as they can be a provider of LNG to Europe, increasing US-based natural gas production. Further, the midstream sector has benefited from its defensive characteristics, including stable fee-based revenues and strong cash flow generation, as well as its ability to play offense by exhibiting cash flow and distribution growth. Performance has also been supported by continued cash flow strength, attractive yields, especially relative to other asset classes, and investor recognition of improved long-term fundamentals across the sector. While there has been some volatility tied to commodity price movements, underlying business performance has remained solid, as would be expected of midstream assets, which are removed from direct commodity exposure.

CEFA:

Where are you seeing the best opportunities to put new capital to work?

Peter Vanderlee:

We believe that in the long term, midstream companies and MLPs represent an attractive investment opportunity as the U.S. continues to cement its status as an energy superpower and exhibit sustained hydrocarbon production increases, which can bode well for high-quality energy midstream and MLP companies, as volumes to be processed are poised to increase over time. We have a healthy exposure to natural gas-oriented midstream companies, and indeed, we anticipate healthy growth in US natural gas production as a result of, one, the AI data center buildup requiring significant and reliable power sources well suited to be provisioned by natural gas. Number two, the increased demand for natural gas in Europe for US provisioned LNG as Europe is looking to further reduce its reliance on Russian supplied gas. And number three, is still growing US economy, lifting demand for power sources in general. The recent events in the Middle East where critical energy infrastructure assets, including LNG facilities, for example, in Qatar, have been damaged, further provide a tailwind for US-based companies engaged in the LNG value chain. Given this backdrop, we continue to see compelling opportunities in natural gas infrastructure, particularly companies leveraged to the LNG transporting, processing, storing, and exports. We see opportunities in large-scale integrated midstream players with irreplaceable assets, and we see opportunities in companies that are trading at attractive valuations relative to their cash flow, growth, and yield. More broadly, the sector still offers a rare combination of income, growth, and reasonable valuations, which is increasingly difficult to find in today's market. In our assessment, midstream stocks continue to be attractively valued. Despite stable and long-lived asset bases, midstream valuations have not significantly re-rated to reflect their improved outlook and continue to trade at a discount to technology and the broader market where valuations are very rich. In fact, with midstream, investors can get exposure to more predictable cash flows at lower multiples.

CEFA:

Peter, how do you see an allocation to a midstream energy strategy like EMO best positioned in an investor's diversified portfolio, and likewise for an investor that is more income-oriented?

Peter Vanderlee:

Midstream energy can play a valuable role in a diversified portfolio for several reasons. Low correlations to traditional asset classes like equities and bonds, attractive income, often higher than both equities and many fixed income sectors, and inflation resilience as revenues are often tied to volumes and long-term contracts, oftentimes containing built-in inflation escalators. For income-oriented investors, EMO is also compelling. The sector in which EMO invests in offers high and growing distributions supported by strong free cash flow and disciplined capital allocation. In short, midstream can serve as both an income generator and a diversifier, helping improve overall portfolio efficiency.

CEFA:

Peter, thank you so much for taking the time to join us today.

Peter Vanderlee:

Thanks for having me.

CEFA:

We want to thank you for tuning into another CEF Insights podcast.

data.title: Netflix: Why The 9% Dip Is A Gift For Long-Term Investors
data.description:
Maybe you have already seen the concept of “hyper-casual games.” Zynga, part of Take-Two Interactive (TTWO), has some. Basically, they are smartphone games that do not have a purpose of very long engagement. They are not taken very seriously, they do not have a story behind them, they are not made to create community nor for anyone to talk about them. It is just a game that you see in an ad, download because it seems fun, play for a few hours, see some ads and/or spend a few dollars and stop playing a few days later (although some have higher retention). Below is an image to give a bit more context.

But going to the central topic of this article: Netflix, Inc. (NFLX) is exactly the opposite of this. Engagement is not only growing, but it is growing with quality. And this matters a lot, because unlike a hyper-casual game that usually people do not even know the name, Netflix’s subcommunities are very engaged and make these users of these niches become “forever” subscribers.

And Q1 showed exactly this. Good results, good strategies. Even so, the market reacted poorly, making NFLX stock fall ~9%. That is why I reiterate my buy rating on Netflix.

Netflix’s Q1: Quite Solid

I will go straight to the snapshot of Netflix’s Q1. Revenue was a beat, EPS was also a beat, but also driven by the fee from the Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) transaction (one-off).

Already addressing the elephant in the room, the market did not like the guidance. The forecast was a YoY growth of 13.5% for Q2, which equals a top line of $12.57 billion, slightly below consensus. It seems very bad, right? But more specifically, the top line growth was 0.5% below the market estimates, it is virtually irrelevant. For EPS the drop is larger, we are talking about a miss of -7% for Q2. The guidance for the full year, although it is a miss at the midpoint, I also consider it a negligible miss and that matters little, considering that if Netflix manages to deliver something slightly closer to the top of the guidance, it already manages to beat the consensus for the full year.

Another point that the market weighed was the chairman's step-down. Reed Hastings will not run again for the NFLX’s board, and as co-founder he is an important figure in the company. And well, I do not think it is an irrelevant factor, he is certainly important for the case, for the culture, vision and so on. But at the same time, I do not think this should have such a large weight to the point of NFLX losing practically 10% of its market cap. I mean, the platform already has a consolidated culture, Reed Hastings has not been the CEO for some years, so even if this impacts at some level the uncertainty of the next years regarding this, I think Netflix is already consolidated enough for us not to have a huge weight on the dependence of a single person.

Going back to the results, I liked the numbers a lot. The YoY growth of the top line was 16%, maintaining a high level. Q1 that seasonally has a solid margin, also improved compared to last year, reaching 32.3%. We are talking here about an operating income of almost $4 billion and more than $5 billion of free cash flow. It may seem “low” given that NFLX has a market cap of ~$410 billion, but here we already have a P/FCF sitting at ~20x.

So if we focus exclusively on numbers, I do not think it is possible to say that Netflix’s result was terrible as the 9% drop makes it seem. It is much closer to a solid quarter, or at least mixed. Top line growth was ok, operating margin was good enough, the guidance was modest but also was not terrible.

Cultural Content, Technology and Optionalities

But what makes me bullish on Netflix is not exactly in the reported numbers, but in how these numbers are being built.

Unfortunately, Netflix no longer reports the number of members, so it becomes more difficult to trace assumptions of total addressable market (max number of subscribers) vs. avg. revenue per user (ARPU). But even so I think Netflix’s shareholder letter is always very rich in information.

And this gave me some insights about content, more specifically, the difference between content. The fact that Netflix has already proven itself in content, I not only already knew but highlighted this in other analyses on Netflix. They have already made several original titles that were not only “cheap” but were also a complete cultural phenomenon. In addition, Netflix has also already been targeting niches such as K-Drama for some time, and has also proven to be very strong in knowing how to create this type of community, that is, if you watch K-Drama frequently or participate in this sub-niche, it is very likely that you will remain a Netflix subscriber.

Going to the results, Netflix highlighted this:

“[...] not all hours are created equal. When members fall in love with a title, they return to Netflix more frequently (which helps retention), and it also ignites fandom that travels far beyond the screen—boosting word-of mouth and acquisition [...]”

And it proved this with its strategies. Bridgerton (the new season) had 94 million views (with Netflix’s fair metric, where 1 click does not count as a view, but rather as if 94 million watched the whole season). Bridgerton had several physical events in many places around the world, real marriage proposals (Netflix mentioned 8 in its letter). This is something truly present in people’s lives, I think it is very difficult that such engaged fans will not continue subscribing to Netflix to wait for the next season, or in the worst case where the user is a fan of only one show, return to become a subscriber. And Bridgerton is just one example, personally, I have never watched it and heard very little about it, so it surprised me a lot that it reached 94 million views and is a highlight, but this only proves how Netflix manages to make huge things for “niches.”

This creates a large moat in retention, and as Netflix itself mentioned, increases acquisition via “word-of-mouth.” Think that a person who is so immersed in this type of show, they do not just watch the show. They want to talk to people about the show, follow the celebrities, follow Netflix on Instagram to see behind the scenes. Besides this retention, I also believe that this creates an optionality in experiences and merchandising. Maybe Bridgerton is not the best example of this, but think of a TV show like Stranger Things, where there were partnerships with dozens of other brands to create dozens of types of merchandising. This creates a new source of revenue and also opens potential for revenue from experiences like parks in the coming years.

Giving the opposite example to prove my point, hyper-casual games cannot do this. Retention is low, cultural impact is low, optionalities are low.

A second point related to this that needs to be highlighted are live events, more specifically the World Baseball Classic that was broadcast in Japan via Netflix and that triggered the “largest day of sign ups” in Japan. This is not just another point to prove that Netflix knows how to do live events or can enter new niches. This also ends up showing that Netflix can invest in this type of business to increase growth, e.g., are we in a more stagnant period? A live event can bring some millions of subscribers in a certain region. And with high retention, the lifetime value (LTV) is also high and ends up compensating this investment.

Finally, Netflix’s statement about technology and the acquisition of an AI company (from Ben Affleck) also makes me more optimistic (InterPositive, check it here). As I have already mentioned in other analyses, one of the most important points for Netflix’s financials is the “additions to content assets,” basically content production. It does not enter the income statement, but as it is a content company, basically it is something that every quarter the company has to spend to renew its content and keep the platform “fresh.” Note that in Q1 $4.8 billion were spent, and even so $5.2 billion of cash flow (but let’s remember that net income was inflated by the WBD fee).

My point here is, think if in 10 years Netflix manages to reduce a bit this additions to content assets. For example, let’s say that AI has made productions more efficient, maintaining the same engagement quality but spending 10% less. Let’s also assume that in a more mature stage, Netflix can also afford to invest a bit less in content because it already has several very mature niches with a very extensive catalog, of course Netflix will always invest in content, but let’s assume 10% less in a mature stage. 20% less in additions to content assets means almost $970 million more in the company’s cash flow. This per quarter (considering the last quarter of ~$4.8 billion). That is almost $4 billion more in cash flow just from efficiency and maturation.

Now let’s organize this thought for the prospects. Healthy subscriber growth with live events and acquisition via other niches. ARPU gradually increasing because engaged subscribers accept price increases (even if it is just inflation). Optionalities being able to further increase revenue growth (think of a potential game subscription, ads, parks and all of that). And still, margin expansion with more efficiency. This is a significant compounding in shareholder value if we think about a period of 10 years.

Risks, Valuation and Bottom Line

Starting with valuation, I believe that the 9% drop opens a margin of safety. Even though since my last article NFLX has been presenting a total return of ~14%, I think the price is still good enough. As I mentioned throughout the article, the ~$410 billion market cap is justified by the quality and the good prospects of the company. There is already a capacity for a cash flow of $20 billion in some time, which gives us more attractive multiples. So skipping a step, let’s think that when Netflix is traded at 20x earnings or 20x FCF, and this multiple is “stable” (without considering re-rating or de-rating) shareholder value will be the proxy of free cash flow/EPS growth, which in my view has everything to remain in double digits for some years.

And in my view, this is also where the biggest risk of Netflix is. There are also risks such as competition, changes in trends and regulation, but in general, I think execution is so good that it mitigates a lot of this. What is difficult to mitigate is market expectations. And Q1 also showed this. For the coming years, the market assumes a very consistent top line growth, above 10% per year, and with EPS growing slightly more than this level. Consequently, Netflix is traded according to these high expectations, which means that if in the next quarter it fails to deliver this 10% growth, and starts to deliver something closer to 6% or 8% in the top line, which would still be reasonable, the market will reprice the stock.

I am not saying that I think growth will slow down in the next quarter, but it is something that can occasionally happen. Think of a more difficult quarter due to FX, with a membership growth of ~5% and ARPU flat because Netflix did not think a price increase was suitable at the moment, this means a low top line growth.

What I am saying here is that even though Netflix is a good company and in my view deserves a buy rating at these prices and will be able to generate shareholder value, the valuation is not that obvious, that is, there is potential for drawdown.

But anyway, I think the market misunderstood Netflix’s Q1 and where the real positive points are. I think it is much more worth it to worry about the quality of the content (if it is engaging or not), with the prospects, with how Netflix is evolving and positioning itself for the future, than with a slight miss in the guidance. That is why I maintain the buy for Netflix stock.

data.title: Southern Company: Hold Regardless Of Upcoming Earnings, Still A Core Utility Position
data.description:
The Southern Company (SO) is one of the largest multi-utility firms in the US. As with most utilities, SO is currently considered overvalued, and even though it is a core utility holding, investors should watch from the sidelines. SO operates regulated electric and natural gas utilities and a merchant power business. While earnings will be consistently competitive with the sector, the overall unpredictability of the stock markets, interest rates, and geopolitics could override the desire to add to safe and secure income-generating utility investments.

Southern Company Business

Southern Company serves 9 million customers with electric customers in three states (Alabama, Georgia, and Mississippi) and natural gas customers in four states (Illinois, Georgia, Virginia, and Tennessee). From a customer count vantage point, SO is about evenly split between the two, with 4.6 million electric customers and 4.4 million gas customers.

Southern Company's regulated electric business includes Alabama Power, Georgia Power, and Mississippi Power. SO generates 46 gigawatts of regulated power, with the most famous being the recently updated Vogtle nuclear power plant. Southern has a robust capital plan to satisfy a growing demand from organic growth, population and industrial growth, and, of course, data centers. SO Management continues to increase its 5-year capital investment budget, now estimated at $81 billion, after increasing the budget by 28%, or $18 billion, since January 2025. About half the proposed investments will support growing energy demand with new gas-fueled and renewable generation, along with investments in transmission and distribution.

There are no new nuclear facilities in the plan, while there are considerations for additional nuclear power upgrades. Southern Co. has been burned in the past with substantial cost overruns and completion delays. The Vogtle nuclear units #3 and #4 led the way with overruns of $5+ billion and a 5-year delay in operation. SO was also attempting to build the first-ever lignite coal-to-gas power plant in Kemper, MS, which included a coal gasification process combined with carbon capture technology. However, after 3 years behind schedule and a $6 billion pre-tax write-off, management converted the facility into a traditional combined-cycle gas-fired facility.

While these two headline projects rattled investors, overall Southern Co. has moved past these projects with new generating projects slated to be smaller and more manageable. SO has received approval to add 10GW of new capacity, with the latest investment revisions including ~36% from purchased power, ~27% from renewables, ~21% from storage, and 16% from gas-fired facilities.

On the natural gas local distribution LDC side, Southern Company operates major distribution networks with Nicor Gas in Illinois, Atlanta Gas Light, Virginia Natural Gas, and Chattanooga Gas. By comparison, Atmos Energy (ATO) is considered the largest independent LDC, with 3.3 million customers. While the political pressure to replace natural gas usage with electricity has faded into the background for now, the service territory of SO’s LDC is not historically considered as a hotbed of forced conversion activity.

Southern Company’s merchant power business, Southern Power Company, manages unregulated power-generating assets, mainly renewable and natural gas power. Southern Power generates 13+ MW from 5 facilities in 15 states and mostly utilizes long-term supply contracts. While not a large part of SO at less than 10% of revenues, the merchant power business is anchored in 10-year contracts, providing a steady $2.1 billion in annual sales.

Regulatory Outlook

Historically, Southern Company operated in some of the best regulatory environments. My personal archives of S&P’s proprietary State Regulatory Environment date from 2006, with the latest ranking below as of Nov 2025. As shown, Southern Co regulatory environments remain highly rated for support of the utilities under the state’s jurisdiction: SO electric - Alabama, Georgia, and Mississippi are in Above Average categories; SO natural gas—Tennessee is rated Above Average, Virginia is rated the highest level of Average, and Illinois is the lowest level of Average. Overall, investors should seek out utilities in the best regulatory environment and search for service territories in the Above Average/1 to Average/1. Southern Company offers one of the best, most supportive regulatory footprints in the sector.

The Wall Street Journal published an article in Dec 2025 outlining the cost of electricity by state. In the article, WSJ offered the following graphic outlining the inflation-adjusted changes in electric utility rates from 2010 to 2024 for all customer categories. In addition, Visual Capitalist offers a graphic identifying the average cost of electricity for residential and commercial customers by state as of Oct 2025.

As shown by the three graphics above, Southern Co. operates in preferred regulatory environments, has offered historically stable inflation-adjusted electricity pricing, and offers competitive electricity rates in the $0.134 to $0.152 range per kWh. In a politically charged atmosphere with utility pricing becoming a partisan hot potato, Southern Co. is well positioned to ride out the political storm. Recently, Alabama Power and the Alabama Public Service Commission agreed to freeze residential rates for 3 years, and the program was approved by the state legislature with the Governor’s approval on April 2, 2026. Even though Alabama has competitive electricity rates, the rate freeze stalls the political issue until well past the 2028 election cycle.

Earnings & Dividends

Even with Southern Co.'s past of substantial project write-offs and cost overruns of the early 2020s, CFRA rates SO as A- for its Quality Rating. CFRA’s criteria include 10-year consistency in earnings and dividend growth. Using the Schwab stock screener, there are 107 companies listed as ‘regulated electric’ and ‘multi-utility’ peers, of which 24 were rated A- and A, and no peers were rated the highest, A+. Of these 107 peers, only 9 have earned a higher Quality Rating than SO.

Reviewing Seeking Alpha earnings estimates, SO is expected to generate $4.56 per share this year, $5.35 in 2028, and $6.10 in 2030. Management is forecasting 7% to 9% annual EPS growth at least through 2030.

Quarterly earnings are due to be released in the premarket on April 30. Non-GAAP EPS are expected to be $1.21, flat with last year’s $1.23. In Feb, management offered guidance for the 1st quarter at $1.20, below the then-consensus of $1.27.

Southern Co. has paid a dividend since 1947. With the anticipated late April 2026 dividend hike, SO will be elevated to a Dividend Aristocrat, leaping past the threshold of 25 consecutive years of dividend increases. The consensus is for a quarterly increase from $0.74 to $0.76 for a 2.8% hike. This is in line with its 5-year dividend growth rate of 2.94% - but barely keeping up with inflation. The current payout ratio is 73%, a bit higher than management’s goal of a 65% payout ratio. This implies future dividend growth to potentially be below earnings growth until the payout ratio declines to target.

Risks

The two biggest risks for Southern Co. and its investors are short-term interest rate trends and regulatory environment changes.

As with the entire utility sector, share prices are directly reflective of the current yield environment. If interest rates increase, share prices will decrease. The current yield is 3.12% based on a $2.96 annual dividend, which is below current CD and Treasury debt rates at any data point 1 year and longer.

The bigger risk to investors is a change in the regulatory environment of its primary states of Georgia, Alabama, and Mississippi. AL is under a 3-year rate freeze, and GA approved last year both a 5-year power supply plan and a 3-year customer rate plan. Much of SO’s historic success has hinged on its outstanding relationship with both customers and regulators by providing relatively low-cost, reliable utility services. Consistent operating execution should be the foundation of continuing these shareholder-beneficial relationships. It appears that SO management fully understands and appreciates that state regulators are the sole gatekeepers to utility profitability—for the benefit of both the company and its investors.

Southern Co.'s aggressive capital investment plan of $81 billion over the next five years will likely require significant equity and debt issuance. Southern may need to issue $11+ billion of equity, and debt needs could exceed $10 billion a year to fund management's current plan.

Recommendation

Southern Company is a core, long-term, multi-utility holding for both income and utility portfolios. I have owned the stock uninterrupted for well over 15 years, with my initial position taken in the 1980s. The latest purchase was in Feb 2024 at $67, and this review is my 22nd on Seeking Alpha since 2013. The last SA review of SO was on 8/4/2023 with a Buy recommendation, and the stock was valued at $68 a share, for a 4.1% yield. Since that last review, according to Seeking Alpha, SO has rewarded long-term investors with a 51% total return vs. the S&P 500 at 57%. The investment thesis has not changed - Southern Co operates in the best regulatory environments in the country, has positive underlying growth potential, and offers stable income. What has changed is its valuation and the current unpredictability in the economy and financial markets.

Driven by strong demand growth prospects, earnings could expand by 6% to 8% annually, but they are in line with many of its better-quality utility peers. With a higher current payout ratio, dividend growth will be about half of earnings growth, barely keeping up with anticipated inflation.

The current investing environment is extremely unpredictable, with an overabundance of unanswered issues. Will oil settle at $150 or $40? Will inflation continue to be stubbornly above targets? Will US government debt and budget deficits finally take their toll on the US Dollar? The list of potential resolutions to these conditions, and others, makes the current investment environment too unpredictable, except for possibly speculative traders.

For these reasons, Southern Company is recommended as a Hold, awaiting either better future valuations or an easing of the current economic unpredictability.

data.title: Cohen & Steers: Q1 Resilience Holds Fort For Q2 Recovery
data.description:
Cohen & Steers, Inc. (CNS), managed to stage a pretty resilient Q1, impressive given everything that had been happening in the markets, with the worst of the hits to AUM happening towards the very end of Q1. But the recovery in markets that continues today on account of what seems to be a more enduring ceasefire situation, with the military action stopping for the moment on all relevant fronts, and also the opening of the Strait of Hormuz appearing to be taking place, which can help refill terminals and stave off a more acute energy crisis with oil simply not arriving where it is needed, we don't expect much more pain in CNS financials. We are looking at a downward translation in the yield curve, which is a key thing for the mega-cap-skewed markets but as critical for CNS, which has a lean towards high-duration equity hybrids like preference share products and real estate and infrastructure.

This has always been the focus of our coverage. Reinflation concerns really mattered for these asset classes, and that risk is hopefully going to evaporate with the possible end to the supply shock in oil. On the other hand, AM isn't such a high-multiple industry, particularly with the competitive threat of passive management, and forward multiples are close to 20x, which is higher than peers also indexed to a normalization in markets and rate assumptions. On that basis, we're not interested in playing the recovery with CNS, where any quasi-indexing asset management company is just as leveraged to the broad-based recovery that could come from an enduring end to the war.

CNS Results

The company recently disclosed the latest 3-month results, ended March 31st. In terms of AUM by strategy, AUM only shrank YoY for preferred securities products; the overall AUM growth of under 9% reflects the almost same figure in YoY revenue growth. The rate cutting that started in 2024 permitted recovery in CNS's product classes, namely infrastructure, U.S. real estate, and then the global classes of infrastructure and international real estate. Preferreds were the only class that didn't see AUM growth YoY.

The strongest AUM growth was in the international classes, and this reflects CNS' indexation towards inflation expectation dynamics and the yield curve in general. Infrastructure and real estate are both highly sensitive to leverage conditions in terms of private and public market values since the return on these assets is so sensitive to rates on the significant leverage used for these assets. Global cuts began before and went deeper than the U.S. cuts in rates as the U.S. was dealing with a more stubborn inflation; therefore, the stronger AUM recovery than the U.S. asset classes.

While rate cuts had started in 2024 in the U.S. as well, it did spend a while at a stable rate before starting to be brought down on growth concerns, arguably brought on by the low-hire and low-fire environment as a consequence of AI adoption in the white-collar world.

The Iran War brought on reinflation concerns. The yield curve came up quite a bit, though with the weaker USD outlook, now with money retreating from it as a safe haven to pour into risk assets and move away from the falling yields on Treasuries at the moment, we should be seeing a translation downwards of the yield curve. The upward translation is bad for real estate and infrastructure, but it is especially bad for ultra-long-duration bets like preferreds, which fundamentally are like low-seniority, super-long-term debt, with significant sensitivity through duration to long-term structural rates. With greater geopolitical risks and conflict, manifesting in this case in the Strait of Hormuz bottleneck, long-term rates also came up as war risk and geopolitical breakdown raised structural inflation expectations. Preferreds were hit hard and were a depreciating class since the beginning of the year.

But all their U.S. strategies have performed relatively poorly since the beginning of the year because of the U.S. yield curve's sensitivity to the Iran War and energy price level risks. While reinflation was a threat for all markets, the U.S., which had not completely conquered inflation, was more at risk than most of the developed world. Therefore, the global strategies performed relatively better.

Now, with it appearing that the Strait is reopened for commercial traffic thanks to a ceasefire on the fronts of the War, where traffic recently was still very limited during the U.S. blockade of Iranian ports, terminals can restock on crude products, and a supply crunch is no longer as much of a concern. Crude prices have fallen significantly today, and a sustained end to the war will permit the energy markets to normalize, with the futures curve implying as soon as July.

The declines in AUM since last month to March's end, which were considerable at around 5%, driven mostly by declines in value, should be reversing promptly in line with the broader market's total recovery, also driven by assets that are effectively yield sensitive, namely tech through large horizon values and present value sensitivity to discount rates. The sequential pressure, which CNS withstood well based on the flat revenue evolutions March ended, should feel some relief as questions about whether we're going back to a higher-for-longer situation in the U.S. may start to evaporate, supposing, of course, the ceasefire holds.

Bottom Line

The issue is that, looking at Seeking Alpha data, the forward P/E is 19x on this equity. Annualizing EPS from Q1 gets you to around 20x as well. That's high. We like AM at 10x. That is our rule of thumb for the industry, but it is also very possible to find today in European markets, for example, which are also benefiting from a recovery with the Strait opening. While it's true that some of the fundamentals in the underlying markets, namely real estate, have good space for recovery based on metrics like housing starts, European AM companies, which direct significant client funds into U.S. assets necessarily as the U.S. market dominates the world by market cap and most products are quasi-indexed, also stand to benefit significantly from the better geopolitical outlook as of today. CNS stock looks expensive to us.
